groups.name: Government Agencies
groups.role: National and county government agencies are the primary coordinators of drought response. They set policy, allocate resources, activate emergency protocols, and liaise with international humanitarian actors. Their decisions determine the scale and speed of the formal response.
groups.what_you_receive: Composite risk score and sub-scores, NDMA bulletin summary, KMD seasonal forecast, stakeholder routing table, monthly trend data, and flagged anomalies requiring policy response.
groups.what_you_do: Activate monitoring protocols at Alert level. Pre-position relief resources at Alarm. Declare drought emergency at Emergency level. Co-ordinate inter-agency response through county emergency operations centre.
groups.how_you_share: Issue official sub-county bulletins within 24 hours of national bulletin release. Distribute via ward administrators, chiefs, and NGO partners. Brief media for radio/TV coverage. Use WhatsApp for rapid coordination with county team.
groups.feedback_loop: Submit monthly field condition reports to NDMA. Flag discrepancies between EWS score and observed conditions. Review and validate indigenous indicator data submitted by community scouts.

alert_actions.Normal: Continue monitoring; review preparedness plans quarterly
alert_actions.Watch: Increase field monitoring frequency to bi-weekly
alert_actions.Alert: Activate contingency plan; alert ward administrators; verify relief stock
alert_actions.Alarm: Co-ordinate emergency response; pre-position relief; brief media
alert_actions.Emergency: Declare drought emergency; deploy humanitarian aid; request national support

groups.name: NGOs & Development Partners
groups.role: International and local NGOs provide technical capacity, humanitarian logistics, and funding. They operate in the field, running water trucking, food distribution, livestock off-take, and community health programs. Their pre-positioning decisions at Alert level are critical to reducing harm.
groups.what_you_receive: Risk level, score, reasons, recommended actions, channel message templates (for passing on to communities), and contact information for county coordination.
groups.what_you_do: At Alert: review contingency plans and confirm supply stock. At Alarm: pre-position water, food, and veterinary supplies. At Emergency: deploy field teams; activate emergency cash transfers; coordinate with NDMA.
groups.how_you_share: Relay translated WhatsApp messages to community groups. Support radio stations with script production costs. Train community scouts on indigenous indicator reporting. Operate community information desks.
groups.feedback_loop: Report field conditions to NDMA via monthly situation reports. Contribute to EWS improvement by sharing beneficiary feedback on alert timeliness and usefulness.

alert_actions.Watch: Pre-identify supply depots; alert logistics teams
alert_actions.Alert: Prepare contingency plans; begin supply pre-positioning
alert_actions.Alarm: Deploy field teams; pre-position relief supplies
alert_actions.Emergency: Full humanitarian response; cash transfers; emergency water/food

groups.name: Community Radio Stations
groups.role: Local FM radio stations are the most trusted and widely reached information channel in Samburu County, particularly in areas without cellular coverage. They broadcast in Samburu vernacular and Swahili, making warnings accessible to non-literate and phone-less community members.
members: Any community radio licensed in the county
groups.what_you_receive: Ready-made 30-second and 60-second radio scripts for each alert level, auto-filled with the current risk level and date. Available in English (for translation). Updated whenever the risk level changes.
groups.what_you_do: Broadcast scripted alerts at minimum 3 times daily (6am, 12pm, 6pm) during Alert/Alarm/Emergency phases. Translate scripts into Samburu before airing. Include emergency contact numbers in every broadcast.
groups.how_you_share: Airwaves reach the furthest communities. Coordinate with chiefs to announce broadcast times at barazas so communities know when to listen.
groups.feedback_loop: Report listener call-ins and questions to county government. Flag confusing or unclear message phrasing. Track reach estimates by coverage area.

alert_actions.Normal: Regular seasonal weather updates; no special broadcast required
alert_actions.Watch: Include weekly drought watch segment in programmes
alert_actions.Alert: Broadcast 30s alert spot 3× daily in Samburu/Swahili
alert_actions.Alarm: Increase to 6× daily broadcasts; include emergency contacts
alert_actions.Emergency: Continuous emergency broadcasts; interrupt regular programming

groups.name: Pastoralist Communities
groups.role: Semi-nomadic pastoralist households in Samburu County are the primary intended beneficiaries of the EWS. Their livelihoods depend on livestock and rangeland. They have the most at stake and are often the last to receive timely, actionable information under the current system.
members: Semi-nomadic herder families across Samburu County
groups.what_you_receive: Plain-language alert level (Low / Moderate / High), specific action recommendations, emergency contact numbers, and the next steps for livestock and migration decisions.
groups.what_you_do: At Alert: begin early destocking (sell weak animals). Move toward known dry-season pastures. Register with your ward office. At Alarm/Emergency: implement full destocking, move to emergency grazing areas, report to relief centres.
groups.how_you_share: Share alert information verbally with family, neighbours, and other herders you encounter during migration. Report unusual environmental observations (early plant die-off, livestock behaviour changes) to community scouts.
groups.feedback_loop: Report to community scouts or chief if the EWS alert does not match what you are observing on the ground. Your traditional knowledge is a critical input to the system.

alert_actions.Normal: Continue normal grazing rotation; monitor water sources
alert_actions.Watch: Note nearest dry-season grazing areas; monitor herd condition weekly
alert_actions.Alert: Begin early destocking; move toward dry-season pastures
alert_actions.Alarm: Implement full destocking; move to emergency grazing areas
alert_actions.Emergency: Evacuate to designated relief areas; register for emergency support

groups.name: Intermediaries & Community Leaders
groups.role: Village chiefs, ward administrators, community health volunteers (CHVs), religious leaders, and extension officers are the critical last-mile link between formal institutions and pastoralist households. They translate, relay, and amplify official warnings using community trust and local language authority.
groups.what_you_receive: Risk level summary, recommended actions per stakeholder group, emergency contact list, and radio script schedule so you can direct communities to tune in at the right times.
groups.what_you_do: Call a baraza at Alert level to relay the warning and answer questions. Translate messages into Samburu. Identify the most vulnerable households and ensure they receive the warning. Coordinate with NGO field staff.
groups.how_you_share: Verbal announcements at barazas and markets. Post notices at water points. Forward WhatsApp messages to community group chats. Direct people to *384# and community radio.
groups.feedback_loop: Collect community questions and concerns about warnings. Report field observations to sub-county NDMA office. Submit indigenous indicator observations to community scouts for integration into the EWS.

alert_actions.Normal: Routine community liaison; monthly drought briefings
alert_actions.Watch: Alert community at next baraza; remind of dry-season protocols
alert_actions.Alert: Call urgent baraza; relay action guidance; contact NGO partners
alert_actions.Alarm: Daily community updates; identify vulnerable households for priority support
alert_actions.Emergency: Co-ordinate evacuation; liaise between community and relief teams
